key_insight: Doeblin curves turn a single Markov-kernel coefficient into a nonlinear, level-specific description of multi-way information contraction, giving meaningful guarantees even when the classic Doeblin coefficient is zero.
engineering_relevance: This provides sharper analysis tools for noisy optimization, resilient computation, and privacy-preserving iterative algorithms by quantifying how noise contracts distributions at different divergence and power levels.
method: Introduce a nonlinear Doeblin curve to characterize contraction behavior beyond a single scalar coefficient.
Derive a new variational characterization of Doeblin coefficients and use it to prove bounds on the curves.
Define power-constrained variants and apply them to generalization, noisy circuits, and differential privacy.
evidence_signal: The paper is theory-driven and the abstract reports analytical bounds and applications across optimization, computation, and privacy rather than empirical experiments.
watch_item: The guarantees depend on the chosen divergence/power regime and on kernel-specific bounds, so translating them into tight practical estimates may require careful problem-specific calibration.
